titleStmt.title: Understanding and Improvement of Adversarial Training for Network Embedding from an Optimization Perspective

analytic.title: Understanding and Improvement of Adversarial Training for Network Embedding from an Optimization Perspective

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Network Embedding aims to learn a function mapping the nodes to Euclidean space contribute to multiple learning analysis tasks on networks. However, the noisy information behind the real-world networks and the overfitting problem both negatively impact the quality of embedding vectors. To tackle these problems, researchers utilize Adversarial Training for Network Embedding (Ad-vTNE) and achieve state-of-the-art performance. Unlike the mainstream methods introducing perturbations on the network structure or the data feature, AdvTNE directly perturbs the model parameters, which provides a new chance to understand the mechanism behind. In this paper, we explain Ad-vTNE theoretically from an optimization perspective. Considering the Power-law property of networks and the optimization objective, we analyze the reason for its excellent results. According to the above analysis, we propose a new activation to enhance the performance of AdvTNE. We conduct extensive experiments on four real networks to validate the effectiveness of our method in node classification and link prediction. The results demonstrate that our method is superior to the state-ofthe-art baseline methods.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Graph data play an essential role in social life. However, numerous graph data only contain the network structure, meaning the lack of auxiliary information like node features. To tackle this problem, network embedding methods [Perozzi et al., 2014] have gained rapid progress in recent years. Based on customized definitions of node neighbors, different unsupervised loss functions are designed to project the nodes into lower-dimensional vector spaces while preserving the structure information [Tang et al., 2015;Wang et al., 2016;Du et al., 2018a;Long et al., 2019]. Nevertheless, noises behind the real-world networks and the overfitting problem present new challenges in network embedding.

Adversarial training, initially designed to defend adversarial attacks [Goodfellow et al., 2014], has shown effectiveness in improving the robustness and generalization ability of deep learning models. Thus, it is widely used in the fields of Computer Vision and Natural Language Processing [Miyato et al., 2018]. The basic idea of adversarial training is to introduce adversarial perturbations to data features during optimization, forcing the estimated distribution to be smoother and hence make the model more robust [Miyato et al., 2018]. Tempted by the success of adversarial training in various scenes, researchers begin to apply it to the network embedding area for better performance. However, most of the work focuses on introducing perturbations on network structure [Dai et al., 2018;Xu et al., 2019] or data feature [Deng et al., 2019;Feng et al., 2019;Sun et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2019], leaving adversarial training for model parameters less studied.

Adversarial Training for Network Embedding (AdvTNE) [Dai et al., 2019] is put forward to fill in the gap and has attracted widespread attention. AdvTNE introduces a disruptive change to conventional adversarial training by adding adversarial perturbations to the parameters (i.e., node embeddings) and achieves excellent results. However, compared with the conventional methods that can be interpreted from the perspective of robust learning or function smoothing [Miyato et al., 2018], this new method lacks an in-depth understanding. It is necessary to analyze AdvTNE theoretically and reach a reasonable understanding. On the one hand, although interpretability remains a desire yet challenge in the machine learning domain, it is still important since it will enable better debugging and improvement against the model.

On the other hand, AdvTNE shows great potential in improving performance of models solving transductive embedding problems. A reasonable explanation is in urgent need to exhibit why and where AdvTNE can work well, and thus we are able to determine whether the method can be generalized to other similar scenarios such as word embedding, tag embedding, table embedding and so on.

In this paper, we theoretically analyze AdvTNE from an optimization perspective to explore the reason for its excellent effectiveness and propose an improved strategy EATNE. Specifically, we first prove that AdvTNE can be interpreted as an optimization method. To understand this method more intuitively, we further analyze the relationship between Ad-vTNE and other optimization methods, reflecting that Ad-vTNE is a Momentum-like method that enhances the momentum information during the training process. Secondly, we answer the question of why AdvTNE works well. We discover that AdvTNE shows extraordinary performance when the model parameters get stuck in the saturation region of the loss function. By combining the properties of network data and the relationship between AdvTNE and Momentum, we conclude that AdvTNE avoids vanishing gradient in the saturation region which is quite common in the Sigmoid activation. Finally, based on the above analysis, we propose a new strategy EATNE to enhance AdvTNE for a better solution to the Sigmoid saturation region problem. Empirical results provide strong support for our theoretical analysis and the well-designed activation of EATNE.
The main contribution of our paper can be summarized as follows:

• We analyze the AdvTNE method from an optimization perspective and theoretically give its relationship to the Momentum optimization method. • Through the analysis of network properties and optimization objective, we conclude that AdvTNE enhances the momentum information in the Sigmoid saturation region so that it can converge to a better minimum. • Based on our theoretical analysis, we further improve AdvTNE by designing a new Sine-based activation, which is expected to achieve a better solution compared with the Sigmoid activation. • We conduct extensive experiments on four real-world networks to validate the effectiveness of the method in node classification and link prediction. The results demonstrate that our method is superior to the state-ofthe-art methods.

div.head: Related Work
div.p: Network Embedding Network embedding can be categorized into inductive learning [Hamilton et al., 2017] and transductive learning [Tang et al., 2015]. Inductive learning optimizes parameters of a well-designed neural network, following a node attributes based message passing mechanism. Directly optimizing the node embeddings, transductive learning performs pretty well on the scene of unsupervised learning without node attributes and becomes the focus of this paper. Inspired by word embedding methods in Natural Language Processing, some methods based on the skip-gram model word2vec are proposed, such as DeepWalk [Perozzi et al., 2014], LINE [Tang et al., 2015] and node2vec [Grover and Leskovec, 2016]. Some methods are based on matrix factorization, such as TADW [Yang et al., 2015] and GraRep [Cao et al., 2015].

Adversarial Training Designed to defend adversarial attacks at first [Goodfellow et al., 2014], adversarial training is introduced in Computer Vision and Natural Language Processing for its generalization ability [Miyato et al., 2018].

Adversarial training also achieves impressive performance on supervised and semi-supervised learning scenes [Miyato et al., 2018] for the design of unlabelled adversarial direction. Some researchers focus on explaining its mechanism [Shaham et al., 2018] that it utilizes robust optimization to increase local stability of neural networks.

Researchers introduce Adversarial Training into network embedding for its great success. Different from Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) based network embedding methods [Wang et al., 2018;Hong et al., 2019;Gao et al., 2019], where representations are learnt by minimax games between connectivity generators and corresponding discriminators, Adversarial Training based methods design adversarial perturbations on networks in various ways. Some works apply perturbations on network structures as topology attacks [Dai et al., 2018;Xu et al., 2019], others on node or edge attributes [Wang et al., 2019].

AdvTNE [Dai et al., 2019] integrates adversarial training with network embedding in a different manner that perturbations are added not in input space but in parameter space. Ad-vTNE provides a unique perspective to understand the mechanism behind, which is the main ideas of this paper.

div.head: Preliminaries
div.p: Notations. Considering a graph G = (V, E, A) with the node set V = {v 1 , ..., v N }, the edge set E = {e ij }, and the adjacency matrix A. Here A is defined as
div.formula: A = [A ij ], where A ij = 1 if e ij ∈ E and A ij = 0 otherwise. d i = j A ij is the degree of v i . Given a network G = (V, E, A), the purpose of network embedding is to learn a function f : V → U,
div.p: where U ∈ R N ×r with r ≪ N , preserving the structural property of G. We denote u i = f (v i ) as the vector representation of node v i . Skip-gram based Network Embedding. Skip-gram based Network Embedding (SGNE), the basis of adversarial training for network embedding, is a class of popular network embedding methods, including LINE, DeepWalk, Node2vec and so on [Du et al., 2018b]. In these methods, a vector representation of a certain node v i is learned by maximizing its likelihood of co-appearance with its neighbors N S (v i ) and the loss accelerated by negative sampling is defined as follows:
div.formula: L = vi∈V vj ∈NS(vi) -w ij log S + ij +k • E vn∼Pn(v) [log S - in ](1)
div.p: where
div.formula: S + ij = σ( u ′ j • u i
div.p: ) is normalized similarity between nodes v i and v j under two kinds of corresponding representations u i and u j ′ learned from the model. S - in = 1 -S + in and σ(•) = 1/(1+e -x ) is Sigmoid function, a classical activation function. N S (v i ) is the neighbor nodes set of v i with different definitions in varying methods. w ij is the weight of node pair (v i , v j ) and it can be estimated by the expectation number of co-occurrences of (v i , v j ) for models based on random walk. k is the number of negative samples and P n (v) ∝ d α v is the noise distribution for negative sampling, where d v is the degree of the vertex v and α is a hyper parameter. We set α = 1 for simplicity, meaning that
div.formula: P n (v) ∝ d v . Adversarial Training.
div.p: where x, y is the pair of the input features and the label. θ denotes model parameters and n (adv) is the perturbation on x. ρ acts as a norm constraint of the perturbation n. θ represents the model parameters fixed as constants at current step.

To enhance the local smoothness of the learned function and the generalization ability of the model, the data features are perturbed to the maximum extent by maximizing the loss in each iteration of optimization.
Adversarial Training for Network Embedding. Adversarial Training for Network Embedding (AdvTNE) generalizes the above method to SGNE and obtains function:
div.formula: min Θ L(G|Θ) + λL(G|Θ + n (adv) )
div.p: where n (adv) = arg max
where Θ represents all learnable parameters including u and u ′ and L is SGNE objective defined in Eq. ( 1).

The second term in the objective of AdvTNE, which aims to improve the robustness of learned parameters, directly add adversarial perturbations in parameter space rather than input space as most adversarial models do. As a consequence, this new method cannot be explained by enhancing the local smoothness of the function, arousing us to find a reasonable explanation for the mechanism behind its impressive performance on network embedding.

div.head: Theoretical Understanding of AdvTNE
div.p: Our interpretation of AdvTNE will be divided into two steps. We will start with the optimization process of AdvTNE. Theoretical analysis shows that AdvTNE can be understood as an optimization method. Secondly, we will analyze why Ad-vTNE works so well as a new optimization method in the network embedding task.

div.head: AdvTNE as New Optimization Method
div.p: Eq. ( 3) shows that AdvTNE perturbs the parameter Θ dynamically at each iteration of gradient descent. To optimize the overall loss function, Θ will be updated from both the gradients of L(G|Θ) and L(G|Θ + n (adv) ), indicating the direction of gradient descent changes from only the ∇L(G|Θ). It can be viewed as an optimization strategy in essence. We have the following theorem: Theorem 1. Given an unconstrained differentiable loss function L(Θ), applying AdvTNE strategy (Eq. (3)) with gradient descent is equivalent to iterative optimization based on the following strategy when updating the parameter Θ:
div.formula: Θ t+1 = Θ t -ǫ∆Θ t , ∆Θ t = ∇L(Θ t ) + λ∇L(Θ t + n (adv) t ) (4)
div.p: where Θ t is the value of Θ in the t-th iteration, ǫ is the learning rate, and n (adv) t is the adversarial perturbation which equals arg max n,||n||≤ρ L(Θ t + n).
Proof. Given the parameter at current step t as Θ t , firstly we expect to maximize L(Θ t + n) in the adversarial perturbation generation step:
div.formula: n (adv) t = arg max n,||n||≤ρ L(Θ t + n).
div.p: As soon as n (adv) t is determined, the objective is a function only related to Θ t as L(Θ t ) + λL(Θ t + n (adv) t
We optimize this objective through gradient descent with learning rate ǫ:
div.formula: Θ t+1 = Θ t -ǫ∆Θ t , ∆Θ t = ∇L(Θ t ) + λ∇L(Θ t + n (adv) t
div.p: This updating process can be considered from another aspect: for the original loss function L(Θ), besides the gradient ∇L(Θ t ), we can also utilize an additional term λ∇L(Θ t + n
(adv) t
) to update its parameter Θ t . As a result, it is reasonable to take it as an optimization methods. Thus, the theorem is proved.

Eq. ( 4) holds for every reasonable loss L and it provides the way to update parameter Θ so that AdvTNE can indeed be regarded as an optimization method. Note that AdvTNE updates Θ and n (adv) every iteration following common used manner proposed in [Goodfellow et al., 2014].

div.head: Property Analysis for New Optimization Method
div.p: When the loss function get stuck in the saturation region or the model parameters are about to converge, we have the following theorem:
div.formula: Theorem 2. If ∃t, s.t.||Θ t -Θ t-1 || ≤ δ 1 and ||∇L(Θ t )|| ≤ δ 2
div.p: where δ 1 , δ 2 are small positive constants, then the following inequality holds:
Proof. Applying first-order Taylor expansion on the left term of Eq. ( 7) and neglecting the second-order term, we have:
div.formula: max ||n||≤ρ {L(Θ t + n)} -L(Θ t-1 ) ≈ max ||n||≤ρ {L(Θ t ) + ∇L(Θ t ) T • n} -L(Θ t-1 ) = L(Θ t ) -L(Θ t-1 ) + max ||n||≤ρ ∇L(Θ t ) T • n ≤ |L(Θ t ) -L(Θ t-1 )| + max ||n||≤ρ ∇L(Θ t ) T • n (8)
div.p: Notice that it is a simple dot product between ∇L(Θ t ) and n in the second maximization term, thus the optimal solution of n is ρ∇L(Θ t ). Then we can further simplify the above equation:
Thus, the theorem is proved.
Based on Theorem. 2, we have the following two corollaries: Corollary 2.1. The perturbation in Eq. (3) can be approximated as: n
div.formula: (adv) t = Θ t-1 -Θ t .
div.p: (10) Corollary 2.2. The AdvTNE optimization method can be formulated as:
div.formula: Θ t+1 = Θ t -ǫ∆Θ t , ∆Θ t = ∇L(Θ t ) + λ∇L(Θ t-1 ). (11
div.p: Proof. Based on Theorem. 2 and considering that δ 1 , δ 2 are both small constant, we have
div.formula: max ||n||≤ρ {L(Θ t + n)} ≈ L(Θ t-1 ). Θ t + n ≈ Θ t-1 is the sufficient condition for max ||n||≤ρ {L(Θ t + n)} ≈ L(Θ t-1
div.p: ). Given that we only need to find a suitable solution of n, so that we have:
div.formula: n (adv) t = Θ t-1 -Θ t .(12)
div.p: Replacing the n (adv) t in Theorem. 1, we can easily obtain:
div.formula: Θ t+1 = Θ t -ǫ∆Θ t , ∆Θ t = ∇L(Θ t ) + λ∇L(Θ t-1 ). (13
div.p: These two corollaries have been proven.

From Theorem. 1 we can discover that AdvTNE strategy can be regarded as a new optimization method for the theorem holds whatever the loss function is. Theorem. 2 demonstrates that we can utilize the gradient of Θ t and its previous state Θ t-1 to update model parameter under certain conditions.

div.head: Relationship with Momentum
div.p: To understand the new optimization method more intuitively, we further analyze the relationship between the AdvTNE strategy and classic optimization method, i.e., Momentum [Ruder, 2016]. We first show updating strategy of Momentum:
div.formula: Θ t+1 = Θ t -ǫ∆Θ t , (14
div.p: where η ∈ (0, 1) is a hyper-parameter controlling the weight of historical information.
In particular, Momentum computes Exponential Moving Average of the gradient sequence to run through the saturation region, which can be seen by expanding Eq. ( 15) as:

Eq. ( 16) indicates that weights of gradients at previous states will decrease exponentially by η. Comparing the updating strategies of Momentum in Eq. ( 16) and AdvTNE in Eq. ( 11), it is obvious that they are similar in the parameter update process, especially if O(η 2 ) is small enough and can be ignored. In general, when conditions in Theorem. 2 hold, the perturbation n (adv) could be approximated by (Θ t-1 -Θ t ). Thus, update information is replaced by the combination of ∇L(Θ t-1 ) and ∇L(Θ t ), which is similar to Momentum.
From the condition of Theorem. 2, we can also find that

indicates that when the values of the loss function in two consecutive iterations are very close, i.e., the loss function gets stuck in the saturation region or the model parameters are about to converge, the AdvTNE method shows its superiority over the original method in this scene. This raises another question: is the phenomenon of saturation region severe in network embedding? We will discuss it in the next section.

div.head: Why AdvTNE Performs Well
div.p: AdvTNE, regarded as a new optimization method, decreases the loss function in a way that strengthens the momentum information, especially when the loss function is on the saturation region. However, we still have no idea why the model's performance is improved when the optimization method emphasizes more on this region. In this section, we will reveal the saturation region problem on network embedding if Sigmoid is selected as the activation. Note that AdvTNE can be utilized for all Skip-gram based Network Embedding (SGNE) methods, and we take LINE as an example in the following analysis for simplicity.

Intuition We directly post the intuitive conclusion of our theoretical analysis. In a scale-free network, the similarity of most node pairs learned by SGNE methods approaches 1, leading to indistinguishable embedding vectors of these nodes. It increases the burden on downstream task as it will be more difficult to distinguish these nodes or node pairs precisely. What we expect is to accurately learn nodes representations that can tell slight differences between similarity of node pairs. However, utilizing Sigmoid as the activation in SGNE models will force the similarity of node pairs with high similarity to fall into the saturation region of the Sigmoid function. The gradient of parameters will vanish so that the similarity cannot be optimized anymore. Considering the AdvTNE method, an important reason it works well is that it collects the momentum information to enhance the gradient in the Sigmoid saturated region and is able to reach a better minimum.

We analyze the theoretical optimal solution of SGNE method and come to the following conclusion: Theorem 3. For sufficiently large embedding dimensionality r, Eq. (1) The minimum for similarity of point pair (v i , v j ) has the following form:
div.formula: S + ij = w ij w ij + ( didj D )k , (17
div.p: where
div.formula: d i = j w ij and D = i d i .
div.p: Proof. Simplify the objective of SGNE as:
div.formula: L = vi∈V vj ∈NS(vi) w ij log S + ij + k • E vn∼Pn(v) [log S - in ] = vi∈V vj ∈NS(vi) w ij log S + ij + k vi∈V d i • E vn∼Pn(v) [log S - in ] = vi∈V vj ∈V w ij log S + ij + kd j d i D log(1 -S + ij ).(18)
div.p: Since embedding dimensionality r is sufficiently large, each S + ij can assume a value independently of the others [Levy and Goldberg, 2014]. We consider the local objective for S + ij :
div.formula: L ij = w ij log S + ij + kd j d i D log(1 -S + ij ). (19
div.p: Let the gradient ∇L ij (S + ij ) be equal to 0:
div.formula: ∇L ij (S + ij ) = w ij S + ij + kd j d i D(1 -S + ij ) = 0.(20)
div.p: We have
div.formula: S + ij = w ij w ij + ( didj D )k . (21
div.p: Thus, the theorem has been proved.

Note that it is not so difficult to meet the condition that the embedding dimensionality should be sufficiently large. As is discussed in [Levy and Goldberg, 2014], r ∼ 100 can also achieve satisfactory performance while satisfying r ≪ N . The degree of nodes in a scale-free network follows a powerlaw distribution, meaning the degree of most nodes is very small. The number of negative sample k is usually set as 3 ∼ 10 in practice. ( didj D )k → 0 holds for linked node pairs (w ij > 0), so that S + ij → 1. In theory, we prove the following theorem: Theorem 4. Considering a scale-free network that the degree d of each node follows the power-law as d ∼ P (d) ∝ d -α , α ∈ (2, 3), the number of edges is |E| and the number of negative samples is k. Given a threshold γ ∈ (0, 1), the following equation holds:
div.formula: ) where R = γk 2(1-γ)|E| .
div.p: Proof. For brevity, let t ij = wij didj and D = 2|E|, and the probability can be represented as follows:
div.formula: P (S + ij ≥ γ) = P ( w ij d i d j ≥ γ 1 -γ k D ) = P (t ij ≥ R). (23)
div.p: Following the definition of cumulative probability and the law of total probability, we have:
div.formula: P (t ij ≥ R) = ∞ tij =R p(t ij ) = ∞ tij =R ∞ cij =1 p(t ij |c ij )p(c ij ),(24)
div.p: where c ij = d i d j . Since we use LINE objective, w ij ∈ {0, 1}. Remind that t ij = 0 if w ij = 0, therefore, we only need to calculate the probability when w ij = 1 i.e.
div.formula: t ij = 1 cij ≥ R, which leads to c ij ≤ 1 R .
div.p: Eq. ( 24) can be rewritten as:
div.formula: P (t ij ≥ R) = 1 R cij =1 ∞ tij =R p(t ij |c ij )p(c ij )(25)
div.p: Now consider the summation:
div.formula: ∞ tij =R p(t ij |c ij , D
div.p: ), following the configuration model [Kryven, 2017], given d i , d j and D, it can be reduced as
div.formula: P (t ij ≥ R) = 1 R cij=1 c ij D p(c ij ).(26)
div.p: Remember that the degree follows Power-law distribution of which Probability Density Function is et al., 2009], we have
div.formula: f d (d) = (α -1)d -α [Clauset

P (t ij ≥ R) ≈ 1 R 1 c ij D f c (c ij ) dc ij = 1 D 1 R 1 c ij dc ij |E| 1 f d ( cij dj )f d (d j ) d j dd j = 1 D 1 R 1 c ij dc ij |E| 1 (α -1) 2 c -α ij d j dd j = (α -1) 2 D 1 R 1 c -α+1 ij log |E| dc ij = (α -1) 2 -α + 2 log |E| 2|E| • (R α-2 -1)(27)
div.p: Thus, the theorem has been proved.
According to Eq. ( 22), we can calculate the expected ratio of the node pairs that satisfy S + > γ to the total number of edges:
Here is a specific example. Let |V | = 1000, |E| = 3000, k = 4, α = 2.5, E[ #(S + >0.9)

div.head: |E|
div.p: ] equals to 81.6%. It can be seen that the number of node pairs whose similarity falls in the Sigmoid saturation region accounts for a sufficiently large proportion. The problem of the Sigmoid saturation region is too severe to damage the quality of node embedding. Luckily, the AdvTNE method, which enhances its optimization power by incorporating momentum information can achieve a pretty good result under this situation. Therefore, we believe it is one of the reason that AdvTNE perfor so well and it is also verified in our experiments. Network Embedding Through the above analysis, we can conclude that (1) similarity of connected node pairs whose embeddings are learned through SGNE will concentrate on high values; (2) the Sigmoid-based loss function (i.e., loss function with Sigmoid as the activation σ(•)) in SGNE has a profound saturation zone effect, which increases the difficulty of convergence for the node representations. Thus, the performance in downstream tasks is substantially affected by the imperfect node representations. (3) AdvTNE solves this problem from the perspective of improving the optimization process. Intuitively, the adversarial perturbations provide the "inertial" component that contributes to a faster descent and avoid being stuck in the saturation region.

It also inspires us that could we find a more direct way to solve the problem of node similarities falling in the saturation region? In other words, since the Sigmoid-based loss function brings a profound saturation region effect, can we seek its substitutes for reducing this effect and improve the quality of embedding vectors? Motivated by this idea, we propose a new activation to enhance the AdvTNE (EATNE):
div.formula: T + ij = 1 2 (1 + sin( π 2 W T ( u ′ j ⊗ u i ))) + δ T - in = 1 2 (1 -sin( π 2 W T ( u ′ n ⊗ u i ))) + δ L(G|Θ) = vi∈V vj ∈NS(vi) w ij log T + ij +k • E vn∼Pn(v) [log T - in ](29)
div.p: Here, we denote the node similarity as T + ij between node v i and v j , W T ∈ R r is a learnable parameter. ⊗ denotes the element-wise multiplication between the embeddings of two nodes. δ > 0 is a small constant to ensure that T + ij and T - in is greater than 0. Inspired by [Sitzmann et al., 2020], we apply batch normalization on the term u ′ j ⊗ u i and u ′ n ⊗ u i so that they follows the uniform distribution U(-1, 1). W T is also initilized as W T ∼ U(-6/r, 6/r) to ensure that W T (( u ′ j ⊗ u i )) ∼ N (0, 1) where N (•, •) is the normal distribution. Eq. ( 29) can be viewed as a Sine-based loss function.
To show the superiority of the Sine-based loss function, we compare the learning progress with the Sigmoid-based one. With Sigmoid-based loss function, the optimal can be reached by
div.formula: S + ij = 1 1 + exp(-u ′ j • u i ) = 1, u ′ j • u i → ∞ (30)
div.p: For Sine-based loss function, the optimal is
div.formula: T + ij = 1 2 (1+ sin( π 2 W T ( u ′ j ⊗ u i ))) + δ = 1, u ′ j ⊗ u i = 2 πW T arcsin(1 -2δ)(31)
div.p: In practice, u ′ j • u i is hard to reach ∞. In addition, when u ′ j • u i → ∞, the descent direction ∆Θ ∼ S + ij (1 -S + ij ) ≈ 0, indicating that the parameters almost cannot be updated after certain iteration. However, the range of arcSine function is finite, enabling the fast convergence of the node representation vectors. Besides, when T + ij → 1, the descent direction ∆Θ ∼ δ(1δ) that is much bigger than that of Sigmoidbased loss function. We conduct experiments on real-world network on node classification and link prediction task to verify our analysis:

div.head: Experiment
div.p: • Q1: Does Momentum information indeed help to solve the Sigmoid saturation region problem?
• Q2: How does our new activation perform compared to the state-of-the-art network embedding models?
Baseline Methods We compare EATNE with the following baseline models:
• Graph Factorization (GF) [Ahmed et al., 2013]: GF utilizes a stochastic gradient descent technique to factorizes the adjacency matrix, and it can scale to large networks.
• DeepWalk [Perozzi et al., 2014]: DeepWalk obtains node embedding by applying the skip-gram model on node sequences sampled from the truncated random walk.

• LINE [Tang et al., 2015]: LINE takes into consideration node co-occurrence probability and node conditional probability to preserve network structural proximities. The problem of expensive computation is also alleviated through the negative sampling approach.
• node2vec [Grover and Leskovec, 2016]: node2vec samples node sequences with a more flexible method and balances the local and global structural properties.
• GraRep [Cao et al., 2015]: GraRep learns node embeddings by using the SVD technique to different k-step probability transition matrix and concatenating all k-step representations.
• GraphSage [Hamilton et al., 2017]: GraphSage collects neighborhood information by constructing a local computing graph, and inductively learns node embeddings.
• AdvTNE [Dai et al., 2019]: AdvT adopts adversarial training methods on network embedding by adding perturbation to embedding, which can be treated as a regularization term.

div.head: Experiment Settings
div.p: We apply AdvTNE and EATNE on DeepWalk and the walk length, walks per node, window size, negative size, regularization strength and batch size are set to 40, 1, 5, 5, 1 and 2048, respectively. The dimension of embedding vectors is 128 and we training each model for 100 epochs. We utilize the recommended hyper-parameters and optimization methods of baseline models mentioned in the corresponding paper. Random search is applied to find the optimal adversarial noise level ǫ, regularization factor λ and learning rate.

We apply random search strategy [Bergstra and Bengio, 2012] to find the best hyperparameters for EATNE. The search range for adversarial noise level ǫ is 0.1∼5. We set 1e-5∼1 as the search range for learning rate and 1e-2∼1e3 for the regularization factor λ. The details of hyperparameters of models for each question will be introduced in the following two section.

div.head: Down-stream Task
div.p: We select link prediction and node classification as the down-stream task to evaluate the quality of representation vectors. The experiment results are averaged over ten runs on both tasks. In link prediction, 80% of the edges are randomly sampled as the positive training samples with the same amount of negative training samples, i.e., node pair without direct edge. The rest 20% edges together with two times of negative samples are sampled to construct the test set. AUC is adopted to measure the performance of link prediction as we train an L 2 -SVM classifier to predict links with the edge features like other works [Dai et al., 2019].

Here we use the Hadamard product of representation vectors from the endpoints of the same edge as the edge features. As for node classification, 18 training ratios, ranging from 1% ∼ 9% and 10% ∼ 90%, are set to train the support vector classifier. It is designed to demonstrate the ability under different proportions of training data so we utilize accuracy as the metric.

div.head: Power of the Momentum Information (Q1)
div.p: To support our claim that AdvTNE is a Momemtum-like optimization method, we compare two models on the node classification task: AdvtNE optimized with SGD (AS) and Non-AdvTNE with Momentum (NM, i.e., DeepWalk only). The experiments are conduct on Cora, Wiki and Citeseer. The adversarial noise level ǫ is 0.9 for Cora, 1.1 for Citeseer and 0.6 for Wiki, respectively. We set 0.001 as learning rate for AS following the original paper. For NM, learning rate is 0.1040 for Wiki, 0.0276 for Citeseer and 0.4524 for Cora. The regularization factor λ is 1.0 for both models. As shown in Table.2, NM can achieve similar results compared with AS. We can also discover that NM surpass AS in the low training ratio case, demonstrating the strong generalization ability of Momentum in the semi-supervised learning scenario. Link Prediction Link prediction plays an essential role in real-world applications, and we carry out this task on four The results are summarized on Table .6. As we can see that EATNE achieves state-of-the-art results on all datasets compared with other methods. Specifically, the AUC score increases by 2.63% on Cora, 6.67% on Citeseer, 8.22% on Wiki and 8.46% on PubMed. Besides, EATNE also performs better than others on large-scale datasets PubMed, proving its value on massive data scene. Node Classification In this section, we conduct a multi-class classification on three datasets. The results are summarized on Table .3 -5. It can be observed that EATNE achieves outstanding performance, especially the best average accuracy on 18 training ratios on all of the three datasets. It raises the average accuracy of node classification on Cora by 1.79%, Wiki by 2.26%, and Citeseer by 1.73% compared with Ad-vTNE.

div.head: Excellent results achieved by EATNE (Q2)
div.p: Note that although Cora and Citeseer are similar in network scale and number of classes, the imbalanced class distribution contributes to higher accuracy in Cora than in Citeseer. By the way, we utilize random features as an input of GraphSage to carry on experiments under the network scene without node features like other methods.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: In this paper, we study AdvTNE theoretically from an optimization perspective, explore the reason for its excellent effectiveness, and propose a new activation based on AdvTNE.

We first prove that AdvTNE can be interpreted as an optimization method and analyze its relationship with Momentum. One reason for the outstanding performance of AdvTNE is that it provides momentum information to accelerate optimization on the saturation region. To address the saturation region problem caused by Sigmoid activation, we design a new activation to obtain high-quality representations more easily. Detailed experiments verify the similarity between AdvTNE and Momentum on node classification tasks. Our method, EATNE, achieves satisfactory results on four data sets on both node classification and link prediction tasks, which practically demonstrates the superiority of our activation. Furthermore, the newly design Sine-based activation is expected to address such limitations brought by Sigmoidbased activation in other similar scenarios. We will apply Ad-vTNE as a new optimization method to solve machine learning problems as well as the new activation to the Sigmoid saturation region problem in the aforementioned fields in future work.

row.cell: Table 3: Accuracy(%) of multi-class classification on Cora
Table 4: Accuracy(%) of multi-class classification on Citeseer

figure.head: Table 5 :
figure.figDesc: Accuracy(%) of multi-class classification on Wiki
